summary: On February 1, 1960, I was in my hometown, Greensboro, North Carolina, when I heard on the radio about four students from A&T college sitting at the Woolworth's lunch counter demanding service. This event sparked the sit-in movement that swept the country. Greensboro was also the place where my cousin's ex was murdered by Ku Klux Klan bullets years later, marking it as a site of both historic protest and personal tragedy for me.

entity: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
summary: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University, or A&T, was central to the civil rights movement when four of its students staged the famous sit-in at Woolworth's in Greensboro in 1960. I was listening to the radio that day as the arrests were announced, and this act of courage ignited a nationwide sit-in movement involving over 50,000 students by June of that year. A&T's students were true pioneers in challenging segregation.

Woolworth's in Greensboro was the site of a pivotal moment in civil rights history when four A&T students sat at the lunch counter on February 1, 1960, demanding service. I was in my car listening to the radio as the police prepared to make arrests, and this event sparked the nationwide sit-in movement. Later, in Philadelphia, my friend Prathia Hall and I picketed at a Woolworth's every Saturday, continuing the fight against segregation in these stores.

The sit-in movement began on February 1, 1960, when four students from A&T college sat at the Woolworth's lunch counter in Greensboro, sparking a wave of nonviolent protests across the country. By June that year, over 50,000 students were involved. I witnessed this movement from afar while starting a new job in Atlanta and later became involved with SNCC and other civil rights activities, which changed my life forever.

After leaving Greensboro on February 1, 1960, I moved to Atlanta to start a new job. It was there that I became involved with the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee and other civil rights activities. Atlanta became a new chapter in my life, connecting me to the broader movement that was sweeping the South and the nation.

I became involved with the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, or SNCC, after moving to Atlanta following the Greensboro sit-ins. I attended the conference in North Carolina where SNCC was formed alongside my friend Prathia Hall. SNCC played a crucial role in organizing protests and projects, including our work years later in Southwest Georgia.

I was a senior at Southern University Laboratory School when the sit-in movement began. Two months after the Greensboro sit-ins, students from Southern University started their own sit-ins, which led to the first mass march by Black college students. My best friend at the Lab School was Hubert Brown, the younger brother of Ed Brown, one of the leaders of those protests.
cues: first mass march by Black students

summary: Southern University students began sit-ins shortly after the Greensboro protests, leading to the first mass march by Black college students. This movement was led by Major Johns, Marvin Robinson, and Ed Brown. As a senior at Southern University Laboratory School, I was closely connected to this activism through my classmates and friends.
cues: leaders Major Johns, Marvin Robinson, Ed Brown

summary: Major Johns was one of the leaders of the Southern University student sit-ins that began two months after the Greensboro protests. Alongside Marvin Robinson and Ed Brown, he helped organize the first mass march by Black college students. I knew of his leadership through my time at Southern University Laboratory School and the activism happening around me.

Marvin Robinson was a key leader in the Southern University sit-ins that followed the Greensboro protests. He, along with Major Johns and Ed Brown, led the first mass march by Black college students. His role was significant in the early civil rights activism that I witnessed while at Southern University Laboratory School.

Ed Brown was one of the leaders of the Southern University student sit-ins and the first mass march by Black college students. I was classmates with his younger brother, Hubert Brown, at Southern University Laboratory School, which gave me a personal connection to Ed and the movement. Their leadership was inspiring during those pivotal months in 1960.

Hubert Brown was my best friend and classmate at Southern University Laboratory School. He was the younger brother of Ed Brown, one of the leaders of the Southern University sit-ins. Through Hubert, I felt closely connected to the student activism and civil rights efforts happening around us in 1960.

I was a junior at Temple University in Philadelphia when the Greensboro sit-ins happened. Watching the news on TV that day deeply affected me, and I was already involved in local civil rights activities. Later, my friend Prathia Hall and I picketed at a Philadelphia Woolworth's every Saturday, continuing our commitment to the movement while still students at Temple.

Philadelphia was where I attended Temple University and engaged in civil rights activism. After the Greensboro sit-ins, my friend Prathia Hall and I regularly picketed at a Woolworth's store there. The city was an important place for me to develop my commitment to the movement before moving South to work on projects in Southwest Georgia.

Prathia Hall was my friend from high school and Temple University who shared my passion for civil rights. Together, we picketed at Woolworth's in Philadelphia every Saturday after the Greensboro sit-ins. We also attended the conference in North Carolina where SNCC was formed and later worked together in the Southwest Georgia project, which profoundly changed our lives.

North Carolina was my home state and the place where the historic Greensboro sit-ins took place on February 1, 1960. It was also where I attended Southern University Laboratory School and where the Ku Klux Klan committed violent acts against my family. The state’s Jim Crow laws shaped much of the struggle and resistance that defined my early life and activism.

The Ku Klux Klan was a violent presence in my life, responsible for murdering my cousin’s ex in North Carolina years after the sit-ins. Their terror was part of the oppressive system of Jim Crow that we fought against during the civil rights movement. Their actions reminded me of the dangers we faced while pushing for justice and equality.

World War II was part of my early life backdrop, a time when I was growing up amid global conflict. I remember discussions equating neutralism with genocide and the tensions of the era. The war shaped the world I was born into before the civil rights movement took center stage in my life.

The Korean War was another major event during my youth, following World War II. It added to the tense atmosphere of the times, with federal agents security-checking my father and neighbors shunning us despite his major promotion. These wars framed the backdrop of my childhood before the civil rights struggles became my focus.

Moscow was a distant symbol of the Cold War fears I grew up with, especially as I read nuclear war comics depicting mushroom clouds over the city. These images represented the global tensions and fears of nuclear annihilation during my youth. They contrasted sharply with the personal and local struggles I faced in the civil rights movement.

Chelyabinsk was another faraway place featured in the nuclear war comics I read as a child, symbolizing the terrifying potential of atomic warfare. The idea of its cremation in those stories haunted the era I grew up in. These fears were part of the larger context of my youth before I found purpose in civil rights activism.

Jim Crow laws were the oppressive system that shaped my life growing up in North Carolina. We fought against these laws through sit-ins, marches, and protests like those at Woolworth's. The struggle to dismantle Jim Crow was central to my generation’s fight for justice and equality.

The Woolworth's Greensboro sit-ins on February 1, 1960, were a defining moment in my life and the civil rights movement. I was listening to the radio as the four A&T students sat at the lunch counter demanding service, sparking a nationwide movement. This event inspired me and thousands of others to take action against segregation.

Years after the sit-in movement began, Prathia Hall and I came South to work on the Southwest Georgia project with SNCC. This work was part of the broader civil rights struggle to challenge segregation and injustice in the Deep South. Our lives were forever changed by the experiences and challenges we faced there.
